title: The Original DDPM Formulation
concept: DDPM was the landmark 2020 paper that formalized the modern forward-noise/reverse-denoise diffusion framework used across the field today.

exercises.prompt: When you drop a splash of ink into a glass of water, what happens over time?

options.text: The ink slowly spreads out until the water looks evenly cloudy
The ink stays in a tight little ball forever
The ink turns back into a solid drop

exercises.explain: Ink spreads out into random cloudiness over time — that's basically what 'adding noise' looks like, and it's the starting idea behind diffusion models.

cards.title: The Paper That Started It All
cards.body: In 2020, a paper called 'Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models' (DDPM) showed the world a wild idea: what if an AI could learn to draw a picture by starting from pure random static and slowly cleaning it up, step by step? This one idea became the foundation for nearly every AI image generator you use today.

cards.title: What Is DDPM?
cards.body: DDPM stands for Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model. It's a two-part recipe: first, slowly add tiny bits of random noise to an image until it becomes pure static (forward process). Then, train an AI to reverse that — starting from static and removing noise little by little until a clear image appears (reverse process).

cards.title: Melting and Refreezing
cards.body: Imagine filming an ice sculpture slowly melting into a puddle — that's the forward process. Now imagine playing that video backwards: the puddle magically reshapes itself back into the sculpture. DDPM trains an AI to do that 'backwards video' trick, but with noisy images turning back into clear pictures.

cards.title: From Static to a Cat
cards.body: Picture a photo of a cat. DDPM's forward process adds a little noise, then a little more, then more, over hundreds of steps, until the cat is completely buried in random fuzz. Then the reverse process starts from that fuzz and, step by step, predicts and removes noise until the cat picture reappears.

cards.title: Myth: The AI Draws in One Shot
cards.body: Many people think image AI paints a picture in a single stroke like a person would. Not true! DDPM-based models actually take many small denoising steps — often 50 to 1000 — gradually refining random noise into a final image, like sculpting instead of snapping a photo.

cards.title: Two Directions, One Model
cards.body: DDPM has two opposite processes working together: one destroys information by adding noise, and the other rebuilds information by removing it. The AI only needs to learn the rebuilding direction really well.

left.text: Clear image → add noise again and again → pure random static

right.text: Pure random static → remove noise again and again → clear image

exercises.prompt: What does the DDPM forward process do to an image?

options.text: Gradually adds random noise until the image becomes static
Compresses the image into a smaller file
Removes noise to reveal a clear picture

exercises.explain: The forward process is the 'destroying' half of DDPM — it slowly buries a clear image in random noise over many small steps.

exercises.prompt: In DDPM, the reverse process creates an image in a single instant step, not gradually.
exercises.explain: False! The reverse process happens gradually, over many small denoising steps, slowly turning static into a clear image.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: DDPM stands for Denoising Diffusion ___ Model.
exercises.explain: DDPM = Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model, the 2020 paper that formalized this now-famous approach to generating images.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes why DDPM was a landmark paper?

options.text: It formalized the forward-noise and reverse-denoise framework used across modern diffusion models
It was the first paper to ever use computers to make art
It proved that noise should never be used in AI training

exercises.explain: DDPM's big contribution was clearly formalizing the two-process framework (add noise, then learn to remove it) that today's diffusion models still build on.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Diffusion models like DDPM generate images by starting with a blank white canvas and painting details on top, just like a human artist.' What's wrong with this statement?

options.text: Nothing is wrong — that's exactly how DDPM works
DDPM actually starts from pure random noise (static), not a blank canvas, and removes noise step by step to reveal the image
DDPM doesn't generate images at all, only text
DDPM paints the whole image in one single step like a photo

exercises.explain: DDPM begins with pure random noise — not a blank canvas — and gradually denoises it over many steps to reveal a final image, which is very different from how a human painter starts and works.
